title: Which suffix should identify std::weak_ptr?
summary: Resolved in favor of T^?: a neutral non-owning caret followed by the selected gold may-be-absent marker, with color intentionally disambiguating overlapping symbols.
details: Unique and shared ownership both use T^, so weak pointers need a distinct suffix that communicates non-ownership and possible expiration.
The caret-question profile uses the established pointer caret plus the selected gold optional marker; tilde-question makes non-ownership more visually distinct; caret-w labels weakness explicitly.
All candidates are two columns wide and buffer-local, so parameter alignment and simultaneous comparison remain deterministic.

Nested suffix expressions use structural parentheses where omission would collapse different source types to the same display, for example optional<weak_ptr<T>> becomes (T^?)? in the caret-question profile.
The owner selected caret-question after a second review and explicitly accepted symbol overlap because color encodes the semantic difference; DEC-016 records the production rule.

provenance.note: Owner requested symmetric unique, weak, and optional handling and examples to judge as usual.
The recursive grammar and buffer-local marker profiles own the compared behavior.

evidence.claim: All marker candidates are verified for exact text, equal widths, RGB roles, and source integrity.
The 5x5 wrapper-pair corpus and real constructor interactions pin recursive weak-pointer behavior.

questions: Which weak-pointer suffix did the owner select?
How are nested weak and optional types disambiguated?

last_verified.method: Owner selected caret-question after a requested second comparison; selection note and catalog hash d590b72d38e937964e3ccd006dfcf12cd65b841705d3c3174e9c38d3b353eead are preserved in style_lab/selections.json.
